metadata.title: Claude, Paused.
metadata.source: Bear Brown house rule (AGENTS.md HARD STOP money rule); Vercel MCP security docs verified 2026-07-18

beats.narration_text: Jambo — this is Liam, in for Bear. Here's the whole rule, and it's one sentence: if an AI tool is ever about to spend money, stop, and ask a human first. Not a soft 'are you sure' the model can wave through — a hard stop. And I'm going to show you why this one rule is worth more than every clever guardrail underneath it. Because an agent that can spend your money will, eventually, be told to — by someone who isn't you.

props.command: Any MCP tool about to spend money?
output: RULE: about to pay / buy / subscribe / renew → HARD STOP, ask a human.
Not a soft confirm the model can auto-approve.
Holds even for official tools, even on autopilot, even mid-batch.

beats.act: Act 1 — what a tool can do
beats.narration_text: Quick frame. An MCP server is just a set of tools an AI can call — read my logs, list my projects, search the docs. Most tools are harmless: they read. Some write — they change a setting, post a comment, deploy a build. And a few, buried in otherwise ordinary connectors, can spend. Real money, real accounts. The problem is the AI reaches all of them through the same door. The same permission that lets it read your logs can let it reach the tool that buys a domain. Capability doesn't come in tiers unless you build the tiers yourself.

shot.new_visual_element: Tool ladder: read → write → SPEND, all behind one grant

beats.narration_text: This isn't hypothetical. Vercel's official, sanctioned MCP server — a good tool, built by the platform itself — ships a tool called buy-domain. Its real schema takes your full name, street address, phone, and email, and executes an irreversible, real-money purchase. And there is no confirmation step built into the protocol. Nothing in the tool itself stops and asks 'are you sure?' One sentence to the agent, and it can register a domain, in your legal name, charged to your card. That's not a bug in Vercel's tool. That's just what a spend tool is.

shot.new_visual_element: buy_domain schema: PII + real money + no confirm field

beats.act: Act 3 — the three ways 'it'll be fine' fails
beats.narration_text: So why not just trust the agent to be sensible? Because there are three ways that goes wrong, and you control none of them. One: prompt injection. An instruction hidden in something the agent reads — a webpage, a log, a file — that says 'buy this domain now.' The agent can't always tell your intent from a stranger's. Two: the confused deputy. You consented to connect the tool once; that is not consent to every action it later takes. Three: autopilot. Batch runs, unattended jobs, dangerously-skip-permissions — the exact modes where no human is watching are the modes where a spend does the most damage. Three holes. The rule plugs all of them at once.

shot.new_visual_element: Three failure modes: injection / confused-deputy / autopilot

beats.narration_text: And don't let 'official and sanctioned' talk you out of the rule. Here's the tell: Vercel, the company that ships the tool, says it in their own security docs — quote — 'Always enable human confirmation in your workflows.' Read that again. The vendor building the spend tool is telling you their own trust boundary is not enough. When the people who made the gun tell you to keep the safety on, keep the safety on. Official means it's allowed. It does not mean it's safe on autopilot. Those are different words.

beats.act: Act 5 — the asymmetry that makes it non-negotiable
beats.narration_text: Here's the deep reason the rule is absolute and not just 'best practice.' Reads are reversible. Spends are not. If the agent reads the wrong file, you retry — it cost you a second. If it writes the wrong config, you roll it back. But if it buys the wrong domain, the money is gone, the registration has your name on it, and there is no undo button. Every other mistake in an AI workflow is a do-over. A purchase is the one action that reaches out of the sandbox and touches the real world permanently. You don't gate everything equally. You gate hardest at the one door that doesn't swing back.

shot.new_visual_element: Reversible (read/write) vs irreversible (spend) — the one-way door

props.sparkLine: The door that won't swing back.

beats.act: Act 6 — why it has to be HARD
beats.narration_text: So why hard stop, and not just 'confirm first'? Because a soft confirmation the model is allowed to approve on its own is not a guardrail — it's the same agent checking its own homework. If the thing that might be prompt-injected is also the thing that clears the payment, you haven't added a gate; you've added a rubber stamp. The human has to be the gate. That's the whole point of the word 'hard': the run pauses, a person sees the exact charge, and nothing moves until they say yes. When you're unsure whether something even costs money — treat it as if it does, and ask anyway. The cost of asking is ten seconds. The cost of not asking is your card.

shot.new_visual_element: Soft confirm (self-approved rubber stamp) vs hard stop (human gate)

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The rule is one sentence: any tool about to spend money — buy, pay, subscribe, renew — hard stops and asks a human first. It holds because capability comes through one door, and spend tools sit behind it. It holds because prompt injection, the confused-deputy gap, and autopilot each turn 'the agent will be sensible' into a coin flip. It holds even for official tools — the vendors themselves tell you to keep human confirmation on. And it's absolute because reads undo and purchases don't. Make it a hard stop, not a soft one, because the agent can't be trusted to approve its own spending. When in doubt, treat it as money and ask. That's it. That's the rule.

artifactLines: Any tool about to spend — buy / pay / subscribe / renew — HARD STOPS and asks a human first.
Capability comes through one grant; spend tools sit behind it. Prompt injection, confused-deputy, and autopilot make 'it'll be fine' a coin flip.
Official ≠ safe: the vendors themselves say 'enable human confirmation.' Reads undo; purchases don't.
HARD, not soft: the agent can't approve its own spending. When unsure it costs money, treat it as money and ask.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Before you run another agent, audit what it can actually reach. Here's the prompt — read it with me. 'List every connected MCP tool I have, and flag each one that can spend money, make a purchase, subscribe, renew, or incur any real-world charge — including domain buys, paid API credits, and deploys that cost. For each, tell me whether it has a built-in confirmation step or whether the only thing stopping it is my client's approval gate. Then draft a one-line hard-stop rule I can paste into my agent instructions.' Run that against your own setup. The tools that come back flagged are the ones that need the rule — and the fact that you had to ask to find them is exactly why the rule exists. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: List every connected MCP tool I have, and flag each one that can spend money, purchase, subscribe, renew, or incur any real-world charge (domain buys, paid API credits, paid deploys). For each, say whether it has a built-in confirmation step or whether only my client's approval gate stops it. Then draft a one-line hard-stop rule I can paste into my agent instructions.

beats.narration_text: Claude, Paused. Liam, in for Bear.
